task: I need a short note to explain a process change to my team before the next shift. Can you help me draft something I could send in a quick message, and if there’s a risk of people misunderstanding it, point that out so I can tighten it up before I send it?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a short, clear draft you could send:

**Quick message draft:**  
Team, starting next shift we’re making a change to our process. Please make sure each file is fully reviewed before moving it forward — no exceptions. This helps us stay compliant, avoid rework, and keep things moving smoothly. Thanks for making sure it’s done right the first time.

**Possible misunderstanding to watch for:**  
- “Fully reviewed” could be vague if people aren’t sure **what specifically must be checked**.  
- “Before moving it forward” may need clarification if there are **different steps or handoffs** in the process.  
- “No exceptions” is clear, but if there are any **special cases**, you may want to mention them so no one assumes there are none.

If you want, I can also make this **shorter and more casual** for a quick huddle or text-style message.

user_simulator.text:
Close, but it’s still a little generic.

I need it to sound more like a real branch message — short, direct, no fluff. Also, “fully reviewed” is exactly the kind of line people will pretend they understand and then miss a step. Spell out what has to happen, in plain terms.

If there’s a version with a firmer tone, do that too. I don’t want this read as optional or just another reminder.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — here’s a tighter branch-style version with the steps spelled out:

**Team, starting next shift, every file must be checked all the way through before it moves on. That means: confirm the file is complete, verify the key details are correct, and make sure nothing is missing before handing it off. This is a hard stop — do not move it forward if it is not done.**

**Firmer version:**  
**Team, starting next shift, do not pass a file forward until it has been checked for completeness, key details verified, and anything missing corrected. This is not optional. If it is not right, it stays here.**

**Risk of misunderstanding:**  
- “Key details” may still be too broad if your team needs to know exactly which items to verify.  
- “Checked for completeness” could be read differently unless you name the specific pieces that must be present.  
- “It stays here” is firm, but if there’s a backup/escalation path, you may want to add that so people know what to do next.

If you want, I can make it even more branch-like by naming the exact items they need to check.

user_simulator.text:
Better, but still not tight enough.

I need the exact checks spelled out — not just "key details" and "complete." That still leaves room for people to wing it. Give me a version that says exactly what has to be confirmed before it moves: signatures, required docs, IDs, whatever applies.

Also want it to sound like a manager setting the rule, not a memo. Short. Hard line. No extra explanation.

And if there’s a cleaner firmer version that makes it obvious this is a process change, not a suggestion, use that.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a tighter manager-style version you can send:

**Team, starting next shift, do not move a file forward until every required signature is there, all required documents are in the file, and all required IDs/verification items are confirmed. If anything is missing, stop and fix it before handoff. This is the process now.**

**Firmer version:**  
**Team, starting next shift, no file moves forward unless every required signature, document, and ID/verification item is confirmed. Missing anything means it stays put. This is not a suggestion — it is the process change.**

**Possible misunderstanding to watch for:**  
- If your team has different file types, “required” may still need a list so people know exactly what applies to each one.  
- “IDs/verification items” may need to be named if there are specific forms or checks.  
- “Stays put” is firm, but if there’s an escalation step, you may want to say who to hand it to next.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-line branch text** or make it even more blunt.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. Still need it cleaner and more like a rule, not a paragraph.

Give me:
- one short branch text
- one firmer version
- and a version with placeholders for the exact docs/IDs so I can drop in the right items

Keep it blunt. No filler.

turns.agent_response:
Here you go:

**1) Short branch text**  
Team: starting next shift, no file moves forward unless all required signatures, documents, and IDs are in the file and verified.

**2) Firmer version**  
Team: starting next shift, do not move any file forward until every required signature, document, and ID is confirmed. If it is missing or unverified, it stops here.

**3) Version with placeholders**  
Team: starting next shift, no file moves forward until all required **[signatures]**, **[documents]**, **[IDs]**, and **[verification items]** are complete and confirmed. If anything is missing, the file does not move.

**Possible misunderstanding to tighten up**
- “Required” may still be vague unless you name the exact items.
- “Verified” could mean different things unless you define who verifies and how.
- If there are exceptions, people may assume there are none unless you say so.

user_simulator.text:
That works. Clean enough.

1) Short branch text
Team: starting next shift, no file moves forward unless all required signatures, documents, and IDs are in the file and verified.

2) Firmer version
Team: starting next shift, do not move any file forward until every required signature, document, and ID is confirmed. If it is missing or unverified, it stops here.

3) With placeholders
Team: starting next shift, no file moves forward until all required [signatures], [documents], [IDs], and [verification items] are complete and confirmed. If anything is missing, the file does not move.

Only thing I’d tighten is if you have an exact list of docs per file type. If not, this is good enough to send.
